---
title: "Difference-in-Differences"
description: "Parallel trends, event study design, heterogeneous treatment effects, and staggered adoption."
---

## Motivation {#sec-did-motivation}

Difference-in-Differences (DiD) is a design for settings where treated and untreated units both evolve over time, but treatment starts for one group at a known moment. The design logic is simple: compare how outcomes change over time in the treated group to how outcomes change over time in a comparison group.

That "difference of differences" removes fixed group gaps and common time shocks, making DiD one of the most practical identification strategies in applied microeconometrics. The hard part is not algebra; the hard part is defending the comparison group's counterfactual trend for the treated group.

If you need the potential-outcomes setup behind this chapter, review [Selection Bias and CIA](00-selection-bias-cia.qmd). For randomized benchmark intuition, compare with [Randomized Controlled Trials](06-rct.qmd). For local-policy alternatives when timing assumptions are weak, see [Regression Discontinuity](05-rdd.qmd) and [Synthetic Control](08-synthetic-control.qmd).

### What You Will Learn

- The canonical 2x2 DiD estimand and its identifying assumptions
- Why parallel trends is the central identifying restriction
- How event studies diagnose dynamic effects and pre-trend concerns
- Why staggered adoption with heterogeneity can break naive TWFE interpretation
- How modern group-time ATT estimators recover interpretable effects

---

## Setup, Potential Outcomes, and Assumptions {#sec-did-setup}

Let $G_i \in \{0,1\}$ indicate whether unit $i$ belongs to the treated group, and let $T_t \in \{0,1\}$ indicate post-treatment periods. For now, focus on the canonical two-period setting ($t=0$ pre, $t=1$ post), where treatment turns on for $G_i=1$ units only in period 1.

Observed outcomes satisfy

$$
Y_{it} = Y_{it}(0) + D_{it}\big(Y_{it}(1)-Y_{it}(0)\big), \tag{4.1}
$$

with treatment indicator $D_{it} = G_i \cdot T_t$. The target is the post-period average treatment effect on treated units:

$$
ATT = \mathbb{E}\big[Y_{i1}(1)-Y_{i1}(0)\mid G_i=1\big]. \tag{4.2}
$$

### Assumptions {#sec-did-assumptions}

| ID | Name | Statement | Role in Proof |
|---|---|---|---|
| DID.1 | Stable composition | Group membership is well-defined and comparable over time | Prevents composition shifts from masquerading as treatment effects |
| DID.2 | No anticipation | For treated units, potential outcomes at $t=0$ are unaffected by future treatment | Lets pre-period outcomes represent untreated behavior |
| DID.3 | Parallel trends (untreated potential outcomes) | $\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(0)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=1]=\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(0)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=0]$ | Identifies missing counterfactual trend for treated group |
| DID.4 | SUTVA/no spillovers across groups | One unit's treatment does not change another unit's potential outcomes | Ensures control trend is a valid benchmark |

The core tension is DID.3. It is never guaranteed by design in observational settings, so DiD is strongest when institutional context makes common trend evolution plausible.

---

## Canonical 2x2 DiD Estimator {#sec-did-2x2}

Define group-time means $\bar Y_{gt}=\mathbb{E}[Y_{it}\mid G_i=g, t]$. The population DiD contrast is

$$
\delta_{DID}=\big(\bar Y_{11}-\bar Y_{10}\big)-\big(\bar Y_{01}-\bar Y_{00}\big). \tag{4.3}
$$

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 4.1 (2x2 DiD Identifies ATT under Parallel Trends)

Under DID.1-DID.4,

$$
\delta_{DID}=ATT. \tag{4.4}
$$
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 📐 Expand proof of Theorem 4.1

**Step 1 (decompose treated-group change; uses DID.2).**

For treated units (using DID.2 so pre-period outcomes are untreated potential outcomes):

$$
\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}-Y_{i0}\mid G_i=1]
=
\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(1)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=1].
$$

Add and subtract $Y_{i1}(0)$:

$$
=
\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(1)-Y_{i1}(0)\mid G_i=1]
+
\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(0)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=1]. \tag{4.5}
$$

The first term is $ATT$.

**Step 2 (control-group change; uses untreated controls and DID.4).**

Controls are untreated in both periods, so:

$$
\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}-Y_{i0}\mid G_i=0]
=
\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(0)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=0]. \tag{4.6}
$$

**Step 3 (difference out untreated trend; uses DID.3).**

Subtract (4.6) from (4.5):

$$
\delta_{DID}
=
ATT
+
\Big(\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(0)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=1]-\mathbb{E}[Y_{i1}(0)-Y_{i0}(0)\mid G_i=0]\Big).
$$

By DID.3 (parallel trends), the bracketed term is zero, so $\delta_{DID}=ATT$. $\blacksquare$
:::

The proof highlights the only genuinely unobserved object: the treated group's untreated post-period trend.

---

## Regression Form and Event Study Logic {#sec-did-regression-event-study}

The 2x2 setup is the cleanest place to see identification. Empirical work usually extends this logic to many periods and cohorts, where the key identifying question stays the same: would treated units have followed the same untreated trend as comparison units in the absence of treatment?

The canonical DiD regression in repeated cross-sections or short panels is

$$
Y_{it}=\alpha+\lambda G_i+\tau T_t+\beta(G_i\times T_t)+u_{it}, \tag{4.7}
$$

where $\beta$ is the DiD estimand in the 2x2 design, and under DID.1-DID.4 it equals $ATT$. With panel data, it is common to write a two-way fixed-effects (TWFE) model:

$$
Y_{it}=\alpha_i+\gamma_t+\beta D_{it}+u_{it}. \tag{4.8}
$$

In the strict 2x2 case, (4.8) matches (4.3). In staggered adoption, however, TWFE can mix many implicit comparisons, including already-treated groups acting as controls for newly treated groups. For inference, cluster standard errors at the treatment-assignment variation level (for example, state or policy-unit level when treatment varies at that level).

### Event Study Specification {#sec-did-event-study}

To visualize dynamic effects and inspect pre-trends, use relative-time indicators:

$$
Y_{it}=\alpha_i+\gamma_t+\sum_{k\neq -1}\theta_k\,1\{t-T_i^*=k\}+u_{it}, \tag{4.9}
$$

where $T_i^*$ is unit $i$'s treatment start time for ever-treated units and period $k=-1$ is omitted as baseline. Never-treated units are included via fixed effects and as comparison observations, but they do not carry event-time indicators.

Interpretation guide:

1. **Pre-period coefficients** ($k<0$) near zero support (but do not prove) parallel trends.
2. **Post-period coefficients** ($k\ge 0$) trace treatment dynamics.
3. Strong pre-trend drift suggests design fragility; revisit comparison group choice or switch design.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pre-trend tests are diagnostics, not identification proofs

A statistically flat pre-trend does not prove parallel trends; it may reflect low power. Conversely, noisy pre-trend deviations do not always invalidate identification if institutional timing and covariate-adjusted trends support the design.

Use design knowledge first, event-study plots second.
:::

---

## Staggered Adoption and Heterogeneous Effects {#sec-did-staggered-heterogeneity}

Modern applications often have staggered rollout: different groups are treated at different dates. If treatment effects vary across groups or over exposure duration, naive TWFE with one treatment indicator can be hard to interpret.

Key issue: TWFE may place implicit negative or non-transparent weights on some group-time effects. This is why recent practice emphasizes group-time average treatment effects. Let $A_i$ denote unit $i$'s first-treated period, with $A_i=\infty$ for never-treated units:

$$
ATT(a,t)=\mathbb{E}[Y_{it}(1)-Y_{it}(0)\mid A_i=a],\qquad t\ge a. \tag{4.10}
$$

Then aggregate with explicit weights tied to your target population and policy question.

Practical workflow for staggered settings:

1. Estimate $ATT(a,t)$ using not-yet-treated or never-treated comparison sets.
2. Aggregate transparently (e.g., cohort-weighted ATT, calendar-time ATT).
3. Report event-time dynamics with confidence bands.
4. Cross-check inference choices in [Standard Errors in Practice](07-standard-errors.qmd), especially clustering by treatment assignment level.

For matrix-estimation intuition of weighted moments, connect this section to [Generalized Method of Moments](10-gmm.qmd). For a design alternative that directly constructs synthetic counterfactual paths, compare with [Synthetic Control](08-synthetic-control.qmd).

---

## Design Checklist for Applied DiD {#sec-did-checklist}

Before interpreting a DiD coefficient causally, ask:

1. **Policy timing:** Is treatment timing plausibly exogenous to short-run shocks in outcomes?
2. **Comparison group quality:** Why should controls capture untreated trend for treated units?
3. **Anticipation:** Could units change behavior before official treatment date?
4. **Spillovers:** Can treatment of one unit affect outcomes of comparison units?
5. **Inference level:** Are standard errors clustered at the correct treatment-variation level?

If this checklist fails, reconsider design assumptions or use alternatives like [Instrumental Variables](03-iv.qmd) or [Regression Discontinuity](05-rdd.qmd), depending on institutional context.

---

## Interactive Simulation {#sec-did-simulation}

The simulation below generates a simple two-group panel, then compares true ATT with DiD under controllable parallel-trend violations and treatment-effect heterogeneity.

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 580
#| echo: false

# Simulation: DiD bias under parallel-trend violations and heterogeneity

from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

app_ui = ui.page_fillable(
    ui.layout_sidebar(
        ui.sidebar(
            ui.input_slider("n_per_group", "Units per group", min=100, max=3000, value=800, step=100),
            ui.input_slider("periods", "Pre and post periods", min=2, max=8, value=4, step=1),
            ui.input_slider("tau", "Average treatment effect", min=-2.0, max=4.0, value=1.2, step=0.1),
            ui.input_slider("hetero", "Treatment heterogeneity (standard deviation)", min=0.0, max=2.0, value=0.5, step=0.1),
            ui.input_slider("trend_gap", "Parallel-trend violation (treated slope gap)", min=-1.0, max=1.0, value=0.0, step=0.05),
            ui.input_slider("noise", "Outcome noise (standard deviation)", min=0.1, max=4.0, value=1.0, step=0.1),
            ui.input_action_button("resample", "Resample", class_="btn-primary w-100 mt-2"),
            ui.hr(),
            ui.markdown(
                "**Interpretation:** Keep trend gap at 0 for valid DiD. "
                "Move it away from 0 to see how bias emerges even with many observations."
            ),
            width=350,
        ),
        ui.output_plot("did_plot"),
    ),
)


def server(input, output, session):
    @reactive.calc
    def simulate_panel():
        input.resample()
        np.random.seed(None)

        n = input.n_per_group()
        t_count = input.periods()
        tau = input.tau()
        hetero_sd = input.hetero()
        slope_gap = input.trend_gap()
        noise_sd = input.noise()

        total_units = 2 * n
        unit_group = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n, dtype=int), np.ones(n, dtype=int)])
        unit_fe = np.random.normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0, size=total_units)
        unit_tau = tau + np.random.normal(loc=0.0, scale=hetero_sd, size=total_units)

        post_start = t_count // 2
        time_index = np.arange(t_count)
        common_time = 0.5 * time_index

        y = np.zeros((total_units, t_count))
        for t in range(t_count):
            post = 1 if t >= post_start else 0
            treated_trend = slope_gap * t
            eps_t = np.random.normal(loc=0.0, scale=noise_sd, size=total_units)

            untreated = unit_fe + common_time[t] + treated_trend * unit_group + eps_t
            treat_effect = unit_tau * post * unit_group

            y[:, t] = untreated + treat_effect
        pre_mask = time_index < post_start
        post_mask = time_index >= post_start

        y_treat_pre = y[unit_group == 1][:, pre_mask].mean()
        y_treat_post = y[unit_group == 1][:, post_mask].mean()
        y_ctrl_pre = y[unit_group == 0][:, pre_mask].mean()
        y_ctrl_post = y[unit_group == 0][:, post_mask].mean()

        did_est = (y_treat_post - y_treat_pre) - (y_ctrl_post - y_ctrl_pre)
        true_att = unit_tau[unit_group == 1].mean()
        bias = did_est - true_att

        treat_series = y[unit_group == 1].mean(axis=0)
        ctrl_series = y[unit_group == 0].mean(axis=0)

        return {
            "time_index": time_index,
            "post_start": post_start,
            "treat_series": treat_series,
            "ctrl_series": ctrl_series,
            "did_est": did_est,
            "true_att": true_att,
            "bias": bias,
            "trend_gap": slope_gap,
        }

    @render.plot
    def did_plot():
        try:
            out = simulate_panel()

            fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.6))

            t = out["time_index"]
            axes[0].plot(t, out["ctrl_series"], marker="o", linewidth=2.0, color="#4e79a7", label="Control group")
            axes[0].plot(t, out["treat_series"], marker="o", linewidth=2.0, color="#e15759", label="Treated group")
            axes[0].axvline(out["post_start"] - 0.5, color="#333333", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.2)
            axes[0].set_title("Average outcomes over time", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
            axes[0].set_xlabel("Time period", fontsize=11)
            axes[0].set_ylabel("Mean outcome", fontsize=11)
            axes[0].legend(fontsize=8.8)

            labels = ["True ATT", "DiD estimate", "Bias"]
            values = [out["true_att"], out["did_est"], out["bias"]]
            colors = ["#59a14f", "#4e79a7", "#f28e2b"]
            axes[1].bar(labels, values, color=colors, alpha=0.9)
            axes[1].axhline(0.0, color="#333333", linewidth=1.0)
            axes[1].set_title("Estimand vs estimate", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
            axes[1].set_ylabel("Value", fontsize=11)

            stats_text = (
                "true_att = {:.3f}\n"
                "did_est  = {:.3f}\n"
                "bias     = {:.3f}\n"
                "trend_gap= {:.3f}"
            ).format(out["true_att"], out["did_est"], out["bias"], out["trend_gap"])

            axes[1].text(
                0.03,
                0.97,
                stats_text,
                transform=axes[1].transAxes,
                va="top",
                fontsize=8.5,
                fontfamily="monospace",
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.35", facecolor="white", edgecolor="#cccccc", alpha=0.95),
            )

            plt.tight_layout()
            return fig
        except Exception as err:
            fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3))
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.text(0.02, 0.75, "Simulation error", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
            ax.text(0.02, 0.50, str(err), fontsize=10)
            ax.text(0.02, 0.25, "Click Resample after adjusting parameters.", fontsize=10)
            return fig


app = App(app_ui, server)
```

---

## Summary {#sec-did-summary}

| Result | Required Assumptions | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| Theorem 4.1 (2x2 DiD identification) | DID.1-DID.4, especially parallel trends | Difference of differences identifies ATT |
| Regression form (2x2/TWFE) | Correct design mapping and valid comparison groups | Interaction coefficient equals DiD in canonical 2x2 |
| Event-study diagnostics | Stable composition + meaningful relative-time coding | Pre-period dynamics can reveal design fragility |
| Staggered adoption caution | Heterogeneous effects across cohorts/time | Naive TWFE may not equal policy-relevant average effect |
| Group-time ATT workflow | Valid not-yet-treated/never-treated counterfactual trends | Transparent aggregation restores interpretable causal effects |

DiD is powerful because it converts time and group structure into a causal design, but its credibility lives or dies with counterfactual trend logic rather than regression syntax.
